topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy flyers for the new city, with their impossibly distant skyline, lay crumpled on my desk. Every time I glanced at them, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. Liam. He was moving. Across the country. I traced the edge of a flyer with my finger, the paper rough against my skin. I should have been more excited, more supportive. Instead, my throat felt tight, a dry, unfamiliar constriction. I picked up my phone, intending to text him, but the message felt inadequate, a flimsy shield against the enormity of the distance that was suddenly yawning open between us. I stared at the screen, the words blurring before I could type anything coherent. A wave of heat rushed up my neck, making my palms sweat. I quickly shoved the phone into my pocket, the sound of the click feeling deafeningly loud in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

"So, you're really doing this?" I managed, the words sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. Sarah’s smile was bright, practiced, the kind she wore when she was trying to project effortless joy. But when she mentioned the lease signing, a strange tightness seized my chest, squeezing the air out of my lungs. I kept my gaze fixed on the chipped paint of the windowsill, focusing intensely on a tiny imperfection. I wanted to look away, to dissolve into the background. Every casual gesture she made—the easy way she leaned in to whisper a plan, the unguarded laughter she shared—felt like a spotlight illuminating my own inadequacy. My hands started to tremble slightly, and I instinctively wrapped my arms around myself, pulling my sweater tighter as if to physically contain whatever churning discomfort was rising within me.

[story 3]

The silence in the coffee shop was oppressive, magnified by the clatter of ceramic mugs. I watched Maya across the small table as she talked animatedly about her new apartment layout, her enthusiasm infectious. It was a performance I was failing to keep up with. When she paused, waiting for my input, I felt a distinct urge to vanish. My gaze dropped to my hands, noticing how tightly I was gripping my lukewarm latte cup, the porcelain digging into my knuckles. The thought—that she was leaving, that our shared routine was dissolving into mere memory—sent a sharp, unpleasant jolt through me. I cleared my throat, forcing a casual tone, but the sound emerged as a strained rasp. I wanted the floor to open up and swallow me whole, just to escape the unbearable weight of this sudden, irreversible change.
